items.q: Do particles get bigger when a substance is heated?
items.correct: No — the particles stay the same size; they gain energy, move faster and spread further apart, so the substance expands.
items.misconception: Heating makes each particle swell up, which is why materials expand.
items.random_wrong: Heating makes the particles multiply, increasing the volume.
items.why: Students project the macroscopic expansion onto the particles themselves instead of onto the spacing between them.

items.q: Does sodium chloride exist as NaCl molecules?
items.correct: No — it forms a giant lattice of alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions; the formula NaCl gives only the ratio of ions.
items.misconception: NaCl is a molecule made of one sodium atom joined to one chlorine atom.
items.random_wrong: Sodium chloride is made of free-floating single atoms.
items.why: Formulae look like molecule recipes, so students picture discrete pairs instead of a continuous three-dimensional ionic lattice.

items.q: Are isotopes of an element different elements?
items.correct: No — isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Adding neutrons changes the element, so isotopes are different elements.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes differ only in their number of electrons.
items.why: Students treat any change in the nucleus as changing identity; only the proton number defines which element an atom is.

items.q: The electron configuration of sodium (atomic number 11) is:
items.why: It's 2,8,1. “2,8,8” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The bond formed when two non-metal atoms share a pair of electrons is:
items.why: It's Covalent. “Ionic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which state of matter do particles only vibrate about fixed positions?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Solid. “Liquid” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An atom with 17 protons and 18 neutrons has a mass number of:
items.why: It's 35. “17” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many moles are in 22 g of carbon dioxide, CO₂ (Mr = 44)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.5 mol. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
